sections.heading: Red route cases of appeal
sections.content:
Some adjudicators decisions which could relate to alleged contraventions on red routes. You can use these cases to support your case. They will also help you to understand some of the basis on which appeals are won. In all of these cases the appeals by the motorist were upheld. To make them easier to navigate we have grouped them in sections.
The following cases relate to appeals against Penalty charge notices issued by Transport for London. As they are not generally published we have not included details of the appellant but we have given the case numbers.
August 23 – CCTV issued Red Route parking fines ruled illegal by London adjudicators – Adjudicators have ruled that Transport for London breached Government Guidelines by using CCTV to issue parking fines. Tens of millions of pounds of fines may have been issued illegally.  More on red route section. Search case number 2230060716 Commercial plant services vs TFL on London Tribunals key cases.
A panel of 3 adjudicators upheld a previous decision that Transport for London issued parking fines illegally by CCTV in a clear breach of Government Guidance which states that CCTV enforcement should only be used where enforcement is difficult or sensitive and enforcement by a Civil enforcement officer is not practical. Enforcement by Civil enforcement officer of traffic wardens is practical on Red Routes because traffic wardens regularly patrol Re Routes and issue parking tickets. There is a chance that previously issued and paid pcn’s can be claimed back but it is too ealry for there to be any cases.

sections.heading: Loading/Unloading
sections.content:
Case 2070058759 – contravention stopped where prohibited
This case concerned a commercial delivery where the driver parked on a loading bay because he could not park directly outside the premises due to loading restrictions there. TFL had said that only loading or unloading of heavy goods was allowed in the loading bay but the adjudicator said that they were wrong in Law to assert this and that he was satisfied that loading or unloading within the meaning of the exemption was taking place.  The appeal was allowed.
Loading is not only for heavy goods.
Case no 206056698A – Contravention stopped where prohibited
This case concerned a vehicle stopped in a loading bay on a red route. Transport for London Issued a PCN after they had not noticed a loading activity taking place for just under 4 minutes. The driver produced a delivery note albeit dated the day before. The adjudicator was satisfied that the driver was unloading and that this was sufficiently continuous to be within the loading exemption and the appeal was allowed.
If the sign says 20 minutes then that is what you are entitled to as long as the loading activity is continuous.
CASE NO: 207 0155493 – Transport for London
Tower Bridge Road, SE1
This case was simple in that the adjudicator was satisfied that the motorist parked only to engage in an unloading activity in the course of her employment, therefore no contravention occurred.
Transport for London are all too quick to issue Penalty Charge Notices to motorists legitimately carrying out loading activities where permitted.  They are criticised time and time again for this by the parking adjudicators but they just continue.

sections.heading: Signs facing the wrong way
sections.content:
Case no 2100224850 – Transport for London
In this case the red route sign was parellel to the road rather than facing the oncoming traffic as required under chapter 3 of the traffic signs manual para 10.5. The motorist said that he didn’t initially notice the double red lines and when he did he returned to his car and drove off. Had the sign been facing the oncoming cars then he may well not have stopped in the first place. The appeal was allowed.
There are many red route signs that are parellel to the road rather than facing the oncoming traffic.
Below in our members section we show 20 more decisions on Red Routes where motorists have won.
Case 2070073639 – Contravention stopped where prohibited
In this case the traffic management order showed Old Marylebone Road and not Marylebone Road where the alleged contravention took place. Also TFL stated that loading was allowed only between 10 – 4 but the sign showed no such limitation. The Adjudicator said “the evidence appears in a state of disarray and I cannot be satisfied that a contravention took place” The appeal was allowed.
Go to top
 
Stopped to read a sign
Case number 2060286961 contravention stopped where prohibited
This case concerned a motorist who contended that, whilst he was stopped in a bay at a time when it was prohibited that he had stopped in order to read the sign. Although he had taken over a minute to do this the adjudicator allowed the appeal on the basis that he was actually reading the sign. (the motorist had stated that he had difficulty reading) The appeal was allowed.
Go to top
 
Traffic management orders and emergencies
Case no 2060530789 Contravention stopped where prohibited
In this case the driver stated that he was within exemption 9(1))b) of the traffic management order in that he stopped in order to avoid an accident. He was taped sitting in his vehicle for 2 1/2 minutes where stopping was prohibited. The adjudicator allowed his appeal.
 
Case 2070010821 – contravention stopped where prohibited
This case concerned a motorist whose car overheated. His appeal was allowed because the traffic management order granted an exemption where a vehicle is “prevented from proceeding in the vehicle by circumstances beyond the person’s control. The appeal was allowed
If your car breaks down or you have an emergency then most traffic management orders have an exemption. Why did TFL pursue this motorist.?
 
Case no 2060562898 Contravention stopped where prohibited
In this case the driver contended that he had had a coughing fit due to a serious illness. The initial hearing was adjourned so he could obtain evidence which he did and his appeal was upheld.
 
CASE NO: 206 0350845
York Road – SE1
This involved a coach driver who stopped in York Road because he was unable to gain access to the correct picking up point for passengers as a result of parked cars blocking the entrance.  However, the Traffic Management Order under which Transport for London are entitled to enforce did not even relate to the street in which their vehicle had been parked.  The parking adjudicator said that TFL are not always put to proof that they have the authority to enforce a particular location where in this case they had.  Transport for London were unable to demonstrate that they actually had the authority and the adjudicator stated “”The fact that double red lines appear on the road in the photographs of the Appellant’s vehicle is of course not proof that TFL have the power to enforce a prohibition against stopping at this location.  That authority is proved by production of the relevant Transport Management Order.  TFL have had time to respond to the Appellant’s challenge but failed to do so.”  The Appeal was allowed.
If you have received a Penalty Charge Notice for a parking offence it is always worth asking the Local Authority to produce a copy of the Traffic Management Order that allows them to enforce at the location where your vehicle was stopped.
 
Case 2070073639 – Contravention stopped where prohibited
In this case the traffic management order showed Old Marylebone Road and not Marylebone Road where the alleged contravention took place. Also TFL stated that loading was allowed only between 10 – 4 but the sign showed no such limitation. The Adjudicator said “the evidence appears in a state of disarray and I cannot be satisfied that a contravention took place” The appeal was allowed.
Always ask for a copy of the traffic management order.
Go to top
 
Conflicts with TRO’s (traffic regulation order)
In case number MW600 before NPAS, a husband and wife shared a car. The husband parked in the morning in a bay and the wife in the afternoon. The wife had received a PCN because she had made additional payment to stay beyond the time first purchased. However, when it got to appeal the adjudicator noticed that the council’s TRO (traffic regulation order) contained no such prevision, so the appeal was allowed. A TRO must be lodged with an adjudicator by the council, as an adjudicator is unable to determine an appeal without seeing sight of a TRO.
Discrepancies in the penalty charge notice and other statutory notices or delays or problems in the enforcement procedure. This section also includes penalty charge notices not received by the motorist or motorists driving off before penalty charge notice is issued.
 
CASE NO 206 0225650 – Transport for London
Patience Road, London SW1
This case was simple.  The Penalty Charge Notice did not state the date of the Notice.  It did state the date of the contravention but it is a legal requirement that the Notice states the date of the Notice which it did not.  The adjudicator therefore said “It is invalid and unenforceable and I must accordingly allow this Appeal”.
In order to be valid, a Penalty Charge Notice must have both the date of the contravention and the date of the Notice even if they are the same clearly stated on the main body of the Penalty Charge Notice (not the tear off section).
 
CASE NO 207 0159596 – Transport for London
Richard Place, SW1
“Transport for London had produced in evidence what purports to be an Office Copy of the Penalty Charge Notice. However, this copy of the Penalty Charge Notice does not contain details of the amount to pay, the time period within which to pay or reference that a discounted penalty in compliance with Section 66(3) of the Road Traffic Act 1991, on the evidence produced I cannot be satisfied that the Penalty Charge Notice was valid and must therefore allow this Appeal”.
If the Penalty Charge Notice does not state this information then it is not enforceable.
 
CASE NO: 206 0203931 – Transport for London 
Blackfriars Road
Penalty Charge Notice did not state the date of the Notice rather only the date of the contravention and it was therefore ruled invalid and unenforceable.
In order to be valid, a Penalty Charge Notice must have both the date of the contravention and the date of the Notice even if they are the same clearly stated on the main body of the Penalty Charge Notice (not the tear off section).
 
Case number 2070122422 stopped where prohibited
This case is interesting because the motorist specified several grounds for appeal all of which were upheld!
Firstly the PCN was issued by a traffic warden at 18.57 when the restrictions ended at 19.00. The adjudicator said that as there was no evidence of the accuracy of the attendants time piece that he was not satisfied on the evidence adduced that it was with the parameters required to establish the alleged contravention.
Secondly the adjudicator said that the PCN was invalid because it fails to specify the grounds of the contravention. The PCN referred to (contravention code) no 46 but no key was attached telling the motorist what the offence was.
Thirdly the adjudicator had been provided with 2 (alleged) copies of the same PCN which differed. He said this was worrying!
Fourthly the notice of rejection did not set out the adjudicators powers as to costs.
Fifthly  when transport for London received a copy of the appeal from the appellant they had 7 days to provide the adjudicator copies of certain documents including the PCN but they were not received and the time limit had not been extended.
How can transport for London have pursued this all the way to adjudication. It gives a few pointers as to what to check.
Go to top
Penalty charge notice not received by Motorist
Westminster City Council vs Terrance Chase 
This appeal concerned the case of Mr Chase who parked his car on a metre which allegedly showed a penalty time of 62 minutes.  Mr Chase however said in his representations to the council that the car was not there at the relevant time and no PCN was issued.  It was also stated “If a PCN is not properly issued and served then the council could not pursue any penalty [by virtue of section 66 (I) and paragraph 1 (1) (a) of schedule 6 to the Road Traffic Act 1991.  The chief parking adjudicator at the time allowed the appeal on the basis of “Mr & Mrs Chase say that the car was in the vicinity of Melbourne Place but strongly deny that it was on a meter or given a PCN.  The council relied on evidence as shown on the tax disc but details show that information was added to the PCN on 26th April.  It seems probable that this information was transferred from details of a different (submitted) PCN.  The council had provided no evidence that the attendant recorded these details contemporaneously on the street as evidence for the council’s case.  The adjudicator initially awarded costs against the council of £100 to which the council appealed.
Go to top
 
Driven off before ticket issued
Westminster City Council vs. The Telegraph PLc
On 2nd August 1996. TheTelegraph registered vehicle was noted by a parking attendant parked in the Strand with four minutes of penalty time displayed.  The driver returned to the vehicle whilst the attendant was writing out a PCN and the attendant advised the driver to wait for the ticket.  However the driver drove off before the PCN was handed to him or placed on the vehicle.  The council pursued the ticket.  However Section 66 of 1991 Act “requires a PCN to either be fixed to the vehicle or given to the person appearing to the parking attendant to be in charge of the vehicle.  If the PCN is not issued in either of these two ways then it is void.  The local authority cannot pursue a penalty on the basis of a void PCN which is a nullity.  Further cases were quoted of Moulder vs London Borough of Sutton and Burnet vs Buckingham County Council.  Initially the council rejected the appeal and then decided not to contest the appeal and cancelled the PCN.  The Telegraph asked for costs although the PCN was cancelled because it was not validly served so they did not receive costs.
The rules have changed but as long as a civil enforcement officers was not prevented from serving the pcn or had not started the process of issuing it whilst the car was parked then thid case will still apply.
Go to top
 
Enforcement by CCTV
In the case of Bell Vs London Borough of Southwark, a penalty charge notice had been issued by post for a parking contravention. The appellant stated that he had not received the PCN even though it had been posted by the council. Although the adjudicator said that where legislation provides for the service of a document by post service was deemed to have been effected 2 working days after the document was posted, he said this presumption was reputable. The local authority proceeded to issue a notice to owner because they had not received payment for the penalty, but the adjudicator found that they were not entitled to serve a notice to owner because the penalty charge notice had not been properly ‘issued’ under the 1991 act. He allowed the motorists appeal on the basis that the motorist had not received the penalty charge notice posted by the council.
Go to top
 
PCN not issued correctly or motorists driving off
In case number WC6 an appeal by a motorist before NPAS was allowed because the PCN showed the penalty charge in dollars rather then pounds!
In case number MW625 the motorist returned to the vehicle as the PCN was being issued, got into the car and prepared to drive away. A parking attendant grabbed the PCN and through it into the opened window. The PCN hit the appellant in the face! before then falling to the ground outside the car. The adjudicator at NPAS found that section 66 (1) of the road traffic act 1991 had not been complied with, because the attendant is required to give the PCN to the driver as opposed to throwing it!
On one occasion a local authority accepted that a penalty charge notice had not been served by either fixing it to the vehicle or giving it to the person appearing to be in charge of the vehicle. However the local authority argued that it wished to enforce the penalty on the basis that the parking attendant told the motorist that a PCN would be sent to him in the post. The adjudicator allowed the appeal and said that it was difficult to understand how a notice to owner could have been served in relation to a penalty charge notice that the local authority knew had never been properly issued!
Go to top
 
Unnecessary delays and costs
 
London Borough of Lambeth vs. Mr A Wilde
This briefly is the case where Mr Wilde appealed against the issue of a PCN.  His appeal was allowed.  However, the adjudicator said that he would have allowed the appeal in any event “on the basis of the unconscionable delay has occurred between the receipt by the council of Mr Wilde’s representations against the notice to owner and the service of the notice of rejection.  He also awarded costs of £91.00 against the council because of the delay.
Go to top
 
Not perusing a penalty charge notice within 6 months
On the newsletter from the Parking appeals and Traffic service (PATAS) of December 2003, they drew attention to the fact that a local authority had served a notice to owner outside the statutory 6 months time limit. Having not served a notice to owner within that time, the local authority was not entitled to pursue enforcement yet it continued to do so.
Go to top
 
Statutory declarations
In one case a motorist made a statutory declaration on the grounds that he had appealed to the parking adjudicator but received no response, the court made the usual order cancelling the charge certificate. The local authority should have referred this statutory declaration to the parking adjudicator, in fact it issued a notice of rejection to the motorist instead. The adjudicator said that in doing so it acted unlawfully and that the breach of duty by the local authority was a serious irregularity because in issuing the supposed notice of rejection, it unlawfully purported to impose on the motorist a liability for a penalty and a time limit for challenging that liability.
In one case a statutory declaration had been sworn but when the local authority received it from the court, they wrote to the motorist telling him that he had exhausted all the appeal processes and that penalty was overdue! They also said that unless he paid £40 by a date specified, he would then have to pay £125 and bailiffs would be instructed to recover it. The adjudicator said that the letter amounted to a wholly unlawful demand for payment.
Go to top
 
Road Markings and signs
CASE NO: 206  0223316 – Transport for London
Wilton Road, SW1
The Appellant’s case was that he was parked on a single yellow line although Transport for London said that he was parked on a double red line. They were unable to provide clear evidence that he was in fact parked on double red lines and therefore the Appeal of the motorist was upheld.
Transport for London are only allowed to enforce restrictions on red routes they are not entitled to enforce single yellow lines.
 
Mr M Grimwood vs. London Borough of Croydon 
This case concerned Mr Grimwood who inadvertently parked his car in a solo motor cycle’s bay.  After parking he and his wife carefully studied the various signs insitu and concluded that it was ok to park at that time.  The council argued that the bay was properly marked although the parking adjudicator agreed with Mr Grimwood and upheld his appeal.  Hugh Cooper the parking adjudicator stated “the council are under a duty to provide adequate and clear signage of any restrictions.  This, however, is a case of “over-signing”.  He goes on to say that Mr Grimwood was misled [by the signs].
Many of transport for London’s loading bays are incorrectly marked or signed.
Condition of signage and road markings
In a case in Oxford 828 the adjudicator found that although the lines were worn and no longer bright and fresh, they were none the less adequate to inform the motorist of the restriction and the motorists appeal was dismissed. However, in Plymouth 1477, also before NPAS, a motorist mistook a worn double yellow line for a single yellow line. The time plate was also obstructed by foliage. The adjudicator found that the road markings were not in adequate condition and allowed the appeal.
In Milton Keynes case number 329 before NPAS, a perfectly legible sign had been placed near a tree and became obscured by foliage during the summer months. Although the sign was in good condition and may well have been visible in the winter, it was found to be insufficiently visible during August when the trees were in full leaf and the appeal was allowed.
Go to top
 
Yellow lines T-bar’s
In the case of Minier Vs London Borough of Camden, the motorist agued that the yellow line did not comply with the signage regulations because it did not have a T- bar at the point where it met parking bays. The adjudicator agreed that the traffic signs regulations and general directions 2002 (regulations) state that T-bars must be installed at the end of single and double yellow lines and that no permitted variants were allowed however, he also said that ‘the law does not concern itself with trifles and refused the motorists appeal. We would argue that if the markings are not in accordance with the regulations, they are not enforceable and as regards to ‘trifles’ we would equate this to enforcement of silly technical breaches of the parking regulations.
This case is also relevant to red routes as there may well be a T bar missing.
Go to top
 
Disabled bays
CASE NO: 206 0450260 – Transport For London
North Kerb Bridge Place, SW1
“The Appellant parked his vehicle at the location in question and displayed his child’s disabled person’s badge.  Transport for London’s site photographs clearly established that parking for up to 3 hours is permitted at this location”.  Transport for London stated that waiting is only permitted between 10am and 4pm but they provided no evidence to support this and the Appeal was therefore upheld.
Quite unbelievable.
Go to top
 
Waiting for a parking space or barrier.
Case 2070016726 – Contravention stopped where prohibited
This case concerned a vehicle that was stopped on a red route where prohibited. However the driver said that he was waiting for a pre-booked off street parking space to become vacant. His appeal was upheld because the adjudicator said “ this brings him within the exemption set out in article 9. (1) (e) of the traffic management order allowing waiting for the removal of a barrier at the entrance to the off street parking space”.
Always ask to see a copy of the traffic management order.
 
CASE NO 207 0010264 – Transport for London
Old Kent Road, London SE1
This case involved a disabled driver who had stopped behind a parking bay, got out of a vehicle asked the motorist sitting in the car in the disabled bay if he was about to move.  He said he was so she patiently waited with her hazard lights on although he did not move for a minute or two due to the fact that his car would not start.  The adjudicator stated that “It appears to me that [the Appellant] was causing her vehicle to be parked in the permitted box on the red route at the time alleged.  Causing a vehicle to be parked Paragraph 4 of the Traffic Management Order implies more than being parked and must include stopping and moving into a parking space.  That the parked cars should stall and delay the manoeuvre was not something that [the Appellant] could have predicted when she first arrived at the Old Kent Road.”  The Appeal was upheld.
It is interesting to note that the vehicle was illegally parked but was waiting for a parking space to be vacated by another car.
Go to top
 
Mitigating circumstances/ compassionate grounds
CASE NO: 206 523772 – Transport for London
Tooley Street, London, SE1
The Appellant did not deny that the contravention had occurred but put forward mitigating factors to the local authority.  The adjudicator is not allowed to take into consideration mitigating factors but did refer the case back to the local authority to reconsider the “exercise of its discretion in light of the mitigation”.  The local authority was informed that if it did not respond the adjudicator would take the matter as being no longer contested.  Transport for London did not respond and the Appeal was therefore allowed.
Go to top
 
Hired Vehicles
In the case of ERAC Vs Ealing, a hired car received a penalty charge notice. The general rule is that a hire firm may transfer liability to the hirer provided certain conditions are satisfied as set out in the hiring agreement. The council tried to transfer liability for the payment of the PCN to the hiring firm because the address supplied for the hirer was outside the UK. The parking adjudicator found that it was irrelevant whether the address of the hirer was in this country or abroad, the legislation only provided that the permanent home address is given, unless the hirer was likely to stay in the country for more than 2 months from the date of the hiring. The appeal was allowed.
Next: Stopped where prohibited (on a red route or clearway) – Contravention code 46

Thirdly the adjudicator had been provided with 2 (alleged) copies of the same PCN which differed. He said this was worrying!
Fourthly the notice of rejection did not set out the adjudicators powers as to costs.

Fifthly  when transport for London received a copy of the appeal from the appellant they had 7 days to provide the adjudicator copies of certain documents including the PCN but they were not received and the time limit had not been extended.

How can transport for London have pursued this all the way to adjudication. It gives a few pointers as to what to check.

Go to top

Penalty charge notice not received by Motorist

Westminster City Council vs Terrance Chase

This appeal concerned the case of Mr Chase who parked his car on a metre which allegedly showed a penalty time of 62 minutes.  Mr Chase however said in his representations to the council that the car was not there at the relevant time and no PCN was issued.  It was also stated “If a PCN is not properly issued and served then the council could not pursue any penalty [by virtue of section 66 (I) and paragraph 1 (1) (a) of schedule 6 to the Road Traffic Act 1991.  The chief parking adjudicator at the time allowed the appeal on the basis of “Mr & Mrs Chase say that the car was in the vicinity of Melbourne Place but strongly deny that it was on a meter or given a PCN.  The council relied on evidence as shown on the tax disc but details show that information was added to the PCN on 26th April.  It seems probable that this information was transferred from details of a different (submitted) PCN.  The council had provided no evidence that the attendant recorded these details contemporaneously on the street as evidence for the council’s case.  The adjudicator initially awarded costs against the council of £100 to which the council appealed.

Go to top

 

Driven off before ticket issued

Westminster City Council vs. The Telegraph PLc

On 2nd August 1996. TheTelegraph registered vehicle was noted by a parking attendant parked in the Strand with four minutes of penalty time displayed.  The driver returned to the vehicle whilst the attendant was writing out a PCN and the attendant advised the driver to wait for the ticket.  However the driver drove off before the PCN was handed to him or placed on the vehicle.  The council pursued the ticket.  However Section 66 of 1991 Act “requires a PCN to either be fixed to the vehicle or given to the person appearing to the parking attendant to be in charge of the vehicle.  If the PCN is not issued in either of these two ways then it is void.  The local authority cannot pursue a penalty on the basis of a void PCN which is a nullity.  Further cases were quoted of Moulder vs London Borough of Sutton and Burnet vs Buckingham County Council.  Initially the council rejected the appeal and then decided not to contest the appeal and cancelled the PCN.  The Telegraph asked for costs although the PCN was cancelled because it was not validly served so they did not receive costs.

The rules have changed but as long as a civil enforcement officers was not prevented from serving the pcn or had not started the process of issuing it whilst the car was parked then thid case will still apply.

Go to top

 

Enforcement by CCTV

In the case of Bell Vs London Borough of Southwark, a penalty charge notice had been issued by post for a parking contravention. The appellant stated that he had not received the PCN even though it had been posted by the council. Although the adjudicator said that where legislation provides for the service of a document by post service was deemed to have been effected 2 working days after the document was posted, he said this presumption was reputable. The local authority proceeded to issue a notice to owner because they had not received payment for the penalty, but the adjudicator found that they were not entitled to serve a notice to owner because the penalty charge notice had not been properly ‘issued’ under the 1991 act. He allowed the motorists appeal on the basis that the motorist had not received the penalty charge notice posted by the council.

Go to top

 

PCN not issued correctly or motorists driving off

In case number WC6 an appeal by a motorist before NPAS was allowed because the PCN showed the penalty charge in dollars rather then pounds!

In case number MW625 the motorist returned to the vehicle as the PCN was being issued, got into the car and prepared to drive away. A parking attendant grabbed the PCN and through it into the opened window. The PCN hit the appellant in the face! before then falling to the ground outside the car. The adjudicator at NPAS found that section 66 (1) of the road traffic act 1991 had not been complied with, because the attendant is required to give the PCN to the driver as opposed to throwing it!

On one occasion a local authority accepted that a penalty charge notice had not been served by either fixing it to the vehicle or giving it to the person appearing to be in charge of the vehicle. However the local authority argued that it wished to enforce the penalty on the basis that the parking attendant told the motorist that a PCN would be sent to him in the post. The adjudicator allowed the appeal and said that it was difficult to understand how a notice to owner could have been served in relation to a penalty charge notice that the local authority knew had never been properly issued!

Go to top

 

Unnecessary delays and costs

 

London Borough of Lambeth vs. Mr A Wilde

This briefly is the case where Mr Wilde appealed against the issue of a PCN.  His appeal was allowed.  However, the adjudicator said that he would have allowed the appeal in any event “on the basis of the unconscionable delay has occurred between the receipt by the council of Mr Wilde’s representations against the notice to owner and the service of the notice of rejection.  He also awarded costs of £91.00 against the council because of the delay.

Go to top

 

Not perusing a penalty charge notice within 6 months

On the newsletter from the Parking appeals and Traffic service (PATAS) of December 2003, they drew attention to the fact that a local authority had served a notice to owner outside the statutory 6 months time limit. Having not served a notice to owner within that time, the local authority was not entitled to pursue enforcement yet it continued to do so.

Go to top

 

Statutory declarations

In one case a motorist made a statutory declaration on the grounds that he had appealed to the parking adjudicator but received no response, the court made the usual order cancelling the charge certificate. The local authority should have referred this statutory declaration to the parking adjudicator, in fact it issued a notice of rejection to the motorist instead. The adjudicator said that in doing so it acted unlawfully and that the breach of duty by the local authority was a serious irregularity because in issuing the supposed notice of rejection, it unlawfully purported to impose on the motorist a liability for a penalty and a time limit for challenging that liability.

In one case a statutory declaration had been sworn but when the local authority received it from the court, they wrote to the motorist telling him that he had exhausted all the appeal processes and that penalty was overdue! They also said that unless he paid £40 by a date specified, he would then have to pay £125 and bailiffs would be instructed to recover it. The adjudicator said that the letter amounted to a wholly unlawful demand for payment.

Go to top

 

Road Markings and signs

CASE NO: 206  0223316 – Transport for London
Wilton Road, SW1

The Appellant’s case was that he was parked on a single yellow line although Transport for London said that he was parked on a double red line. They were unable to provide clear evidence that he was in fact parked on double red lines and therefore the Appeal of the motorist was upheld.

Transport for London are only allowed to enforce restrictions on red routes they are not entitled to enforce single yellow lines.

 

Mr M Grimwood vs. London Borough of Croydon

This case concerned Mr Grimwood who inadvertently parked his car in a solo motor cycle’s bay.  After parking he and his wife carefully studied the various signs insitu and concluded that it was ok to park at that time.  The council argued that the bay was properly marked although the parking adjudicator agreed with Mr Grimwood and upheld his appeal.  Hugh Cooper the parking adjudicator stated “the council are under a duty to provide adequate and clear signage of any restrictions.  This, however, is a case of “over-signing”.  He goes on to say that Mr Grimwood was misled [by the signs].

Many of transport for London’s loading bays are incorrectly marked or signed.

Condition of signage and road markings

In a case in Oxford 828 the adjudicator found that although the lines were worn and no longer bright and fresh, they were none the less adequate to inform the motorist of the restriction and the motorists appeal was dismissed. However, in Plymouth 1477, also before NPAS, a motorist mistook a worn double yellow line for a single yellow line. The time plate was also obstructed by foliage. The adjudicator found that the road markings were not in adequate condition and allowed the appeal.

In Milton Keynes case number 329 before NPAS, a perfectly legible sign had been placed near a tree and became obscured by foliage during the summer months. Although the sign was in good condition and may well have been visible in the winter, it was found to be insufficiently visible during August when the trees were in full leaf and the appeal was allowed.

Go to top

 

Yellow lines T-bar’s

In the case of Minier Vs London Borough of Camden, the motorist agued that the yellow line did not comply with the signage regulations because it did not have a T- bar at the point where it met parking bays. The adjudicator agreed that the traffic signs regulations and general directions 2002 (regulations) state that T-bars must be installed at the end of single and double yellow lines and that no permitted variants were allowed however, he also said that ‘the law does not concern itself with trifles and refused the motorists appeal. We would argue that if the markings are not in accordance with the regulations, they are not enforceable and as regards to ‘trifles’ we would equate this to enforcement of silly technical breaches of the parking regulations.

This case is also relevant to red routes as there may well be a T bar missing.

Go to top

 

Disabled bays

CASE NO: 206 0450260 – Transport For London
North Kerb Bridge Place, SW1

“The Appellant parked his vehicle at the location in question and displayed his child’s disabled person’s badge.  Transport for London’s site photographs clearly established that parking for up to 3 hours is permitted at this location”.  Transport for London stated that waiting is only permitted between 10am and 4pm but they provided no evidence to support this and the Appeal was therefore upheld.

Quite unbelievable.

Go to top

 

Waiting for a parking space or barrier.

Case 2070016726 – Contravention stopped where prohibited

This case concerned a vehicle that was stopped on a red route where prohibited. However the driver said that he was waiting for a pre-booked off street parking space to become vacant. His appeal was upheld because the adjudicator said “ this brings him within the exemption set out in article 9. (1) (e) of the traffic management order allowing waiting for the removal of a barrier at the entrance to the off street parking space”.

Always ask to see a copy of the traffic management order.

 

CASE NO 207 0010264 – Transport for London
Old Kent Road, London SE1

This case involved a disabled driver who had stopped behind a parking bay, got out of a vehicle asked the motorist sitting in the car in the disabled bay if he was about to move.  He said he was so she patiently waited with her hazard lights on although he did not move for a minute or two due to the fact that his car would not start.  The adjudicator stated that “It appears to me that [the Appellant] was causing her vehicle to be parked in the permitted box on the red route at the time alleged.  Causing a vehicle to be parked Paragraph 4 of the Traffic Management Order implies more than being parked and must include stopping and moving into a parking space.  That the parked cars should stall and delay the manoeuvre was not something that [the Appellant] could have predicted when she first arrived at the Old Kent Road.”  The Appeal was upheld.

It is interesting to note that the vehicle was illegally parked but was waiting for a parking space to be vacated by another car.

Go to top

 

Mitigating circumstances/ compassionate grounds

CASE NO: 206 523772 – Transport for London
Tooley Street, London, SE1

The Appellant did not deny that the contravention had occurred but put forward mitigating factors to the local authority.  The adjudicator is not allowed to take into consideration mitigating factors but did refer the case back to the local authority to reconsider the “exercise of its discretion in light of the mitigation”.  The local authority was informed that if it did not respond the adjudicator would take the matter as being no longer contested.  Transport for London did not respond and the Appeal was therefore allowed.

Go to top

 

Hired Vehicles

In the case of ERAC Vs Ealing, a hired car received a penalty charge notice. The general rule is that a hire firm may transfer liability to the hirer provided certain conditions are satisfied as set out in the hiring agreement. The council tried to transfer liability for the payment of the PCN to the hiring firm because the address supplied for the hirer was outside the UK. The parking adjudicator found that it was irrelevant whether the address of the hirer was in this country or abroad, the legislation only provided that the permanent home address is given, unless the hirer was likely to stay in the country for more than 2 months from the date of the hiring. The appeal was allowed.

Next: Stopped where prohibited (on a red route or clearway) – Contravention code 46

sections.heading: Stopped to read a sign
sections.content:
Case number 2060286961 contravention stopped where prohibited
This case concerned a motorist who contended that, whilst he was stopped in a bay at a time when it was prohibited that he had stopped in order to read the sign. Although he had taken over a minute to do this the adjudicator allowed the appeal on the basis that he was actually reading the sign. (the motorist had stated that he had difficulty reading) The appeal was allowed.

sections.heading: Traffic management orders and emergencies
sections.content:
Case no 2060530789 Contravention stopped where prohibited
In this case the driver stated that he was within exemption 9(1))b) of the traffic management order in that he stopped in order to avoid an accident. He was taped sitting in his vehicle for 2 1/2 minutes where stopping was prohibited. The adjudicator allowed his appeal.
Case 2070010821 – contravention stopped where prohibited
This case concerned a motorist whose car overheated. His appeal was allowed because the traffic management order granted an exemption where a vehicle is “prevented from proceeding in the vehicle by circumstances beyond the person’s control. The appeal was allowed
If your car breaks down or you have an emergency then most traffic management orders have an exemption. Why did TFL pursue this motorist.?
Case no 2060562898 Contravention stopped where prohibited
In this case the driver contended that he had had a coughing fit due to a serious illness. The initial hearing was adjourned so he could obtain evidence which he did and his appeal was upheld.
CASE NO: 206 0350845
York Road – SE1
This involved a coach driver who stopped in York Road because he was unable to gain access to the correct picking up point for passengers as a result of parked cars blocking the entrance.  However, the Traffic Management Order under which Transport for London are entitled to enforce did not even relate to the street in which their vehicle had been parked.  The parking adjudicator said that TFL are not always put to proof that they have the authority to enforce a particular location where in this case they had.  Transport for London were unable to demonstrate that they actually had the authority and the adjudicator stated “”The fact that double red lines appear on the road in the photographs of the Appellant’s vehicle is of course not proof that TFL have the power to enforce a prohibition against stopping at this location.  That authority is proved by production of the relevant Transport Management Order.  TFL have had time to respond to the Appellant’s challenge but failed to do so.”  The Appeal was allowed.
If you have received a Penalty Charge Notice for a parking offence it is always worth asking the Local Authority to produce a copy of the Traffic Management Order that allows them to enforce at the location where your vehicle was stopped.
Case 2070073639 – Contravention stopped where prohibited
In this case the traffic management order showed Old Marylebone Road and not Marylebone Road where the alleged contravention took place. Also TFL stated that loading was allowed only between 10 – 4 but the sign showed no such limitation. The Adjudicator said “the evidence appears in a state of disarray and I cannot be satisfied that a contravention took place” The appeal was allowed.
Always ask for a copy of the traffic management order.

sections.heading: Conflicts with TRO’s (traffic regulation order)
sections.content:
In case number MW600 before NPAS, a husband and wife shared a car. The husband parked in the morning in a bay and the wife in the afternoon. The wife had received a PCN because she had made additional payment to stay beyond the time first purchased. However, when it got to appeal the adjudicator noticed that the council’s TRO (traffic regulation order) contained no such prevision, so the appeal was allowed. A TRO must be lodged with an adjudicator by the council, as an adjudicator is unable to determine an appeal without seeing sight of a TRO.
Discrepancies in the penalty charge notice and other statutory notices or delays or problems in the enforcement procedure. This section also includes penalty charge notices not received by the motorist or motorists driving off before penalty charge notice is issued.
CASE NO 206 0225650 – Transport for London
Patience Road, London SW1
This case was simple.  The Penalty Charge Notice did not state the date of the Notice.  It did state the date of the contravention but it is a legal requirement that the Notice states the date of the Notice which it did not.  The adjudicator therefore said “It is invalid and unenforceable and I must accordingly allow this Appeal”.
In order to be valid, a Penalty Charge Notice must have both the date of the contravention and the date of the Notice even if they are the same clearly stated on the main body of the Penalty Charge Notice (not the tear off section).
CASE NO 207 0159596 – Transport for London
Richard Place, SW1
“Transport for London had produced in evidence what purports to be an Office Copy of the Penalty Charge Notice. However, this copy of the Penalty Charge Notice does not contain details of the amount to pay, the time period within which to pay or reference that a discounted penalty in compliance with Section 66(3) of the Road Traffic Act 1991, on the evidence produced I cannot be satisfied that the Penalty Charge Notice was valid and must therefore allow this Appeal”.
If the Penalty Charge Notice does not state this information then it is not enforceable.
CASE NO: 206 0203931 – Transport for London 
Blackfriars Road
Penalty Charge Notice did not state the date of the Notice rather only the date of the contravention and it was therefore ruled invalid and unenforceable.
In order to be valid, a Penalty Charge Notice must have both the date of the contravention and the date of the Notice even if they are the same clearly stated on the main body of the Penalty Charge Notice (not the tear off section).
Case number 2070122422 stopped where prohibited
This case is interesting because the motorist specified several grounds for appeal all of which were upheld!
Firstly the PCN was issued by a traffic warden at 18.57 when the restrictions ended at 19.00. The adjudicator said that as there was no evidence of the accuracy of the attendants time piece that he was not satisfied on the evidence adduced that it was with the parameters required to establish the alleged contravention.
Secondly the adjudicator said that the PCN was invalid because it fails to specify the grounds of the contravention. The PCN referred to (contravention code) no 46 but no key was attached telling the motorist what the offence was.
Thirdly the adjudicator had been provided with 2 (alleged) copies of the same PCN which differed. He said this was worrying!
Fourthly the notice of rejection did not set out the adjudicators powers as to costs.
Fifthly  when transport for London received a copy of the appeal from the appellant they had 7 days to provide the adjudicator copies of certain documents including the PCN but they were not received and the time limit had not been extended.
How can transport for London have pursued this all the way to adjudication. It gives a few pointers as to what to check.

The adjudicator allowed the appeal and said that it was difficult to understand how a notice to owner could have been served in relation to a penalty charge notice that the local authority knew had never been properly issued!
sections.contentHtml:
In case number WC6 an appeal by a motorist before NPAS was allowed because the PCN showed the penalty charge in dollars rather then pounds!

In case number MW625 the motorist returned to the vehicle as the PCN was being issued, got into the car and prepared to drive away. A parking attendant grabbed the PCN and through it into the opened window. The PCN hit the appellant in the face! before then falling to the ground outside the car. The adjudicator at NPAS found that section 66 (1) of the road traffic act 1991 had not been complied with, because the attendant is required to give the PCN to the driver as opposed to throwing it!

sections.heading: Unnecessary delays and costs
sections.content:
London Borough of Lambeth vs. Mr A Wilde
This briefly is the case where Mr Wilde appealed against the issue of a PCN.  His appeal was allowed.  However, the adjudicator said that he would have allowed the appeal in any event “on the basis of the unconscionable delay has occurred between the receipt by the council of Mr Wilde’s representations against the notice to owner and the service of the notice of rejection.  He also awarded costs of £91.00 against the council because of the delay.

sections.heading: Not perusing a penalty charge notice within 6 months
sections.content: On the newsletter from the Parking appeals and Traffic service (PATAS) of December 2003, they drew attention to the fact that a local authority had served a notice to owner outside the statutory 6 months time limit. Having not served a notice to owner within that time, the local authority was not entitled to pursue enforcement yet it continued to do so.

sections.heading: Statutory declarations
sections.content:
In one case a motorist made a statutory declaration on the grounds that he had appealed to the parking adjudicator but received no response, the court made the usual order cancelling the charge certificate. The local authority should have referred this statutory declaration to the parking adjudicator, in fact it issued a notice of rejection to the motorist instead. The adjudicator said that in doing so it acted unlawfully and that the breach of duty by the local authority was a serious irregularity because in issuing the supposed notice of rejection, it unlawfully purported to impose on the motorist a liability for a penalty and a time limit for challenging that liability.
In one case a statutory declaration had been sworn but when the local authority received it from the court, they wrote to the motorist telling him that he had exhausted all the appeal processes and that penalty was overdue! They also said that unless he paid £40 by a date specified, he would then have to pay £125 and bailiffs would be instructed to recover it. The adjudicator said that the letter amounted to a wholly unlawful demand for payment.

sections.heading: Road Markings and signs
sections.content:
CASE NO: 206  0223316 – Transport for London
Wilton Road, SW1
The Appellant’s case was that he was parked on a single yellow line although Transport for London said that he was parked on a double red line. They were unable to provide clear evidence that he was in fact parked on double red lines and therefore the Appeal of the motorist was upheld.
Transport for London are only allowed to enforce restrictions on red routes they are not entitled to enforce single yellow lines.
Mr M Grimwood vs. London Borough of Croydon
This case concerned Mr Grimwood who inadvertently parked his car in a solo motor cycle’s bay.  After parking he and his wife carefully studied the various signs insitu and concluded that it was ok to park at that time.  The council argued that the bay was properly marked although the parking adjudicator agreed with Mr Grimwood and upheld his appeal.  Hugh Cooper the parking adjudicator stated “the council are under a duty to provide adequate and clear signage of any restrictions.  This, however, is a case of “over-signing”.  He goes on to say that Mr Grimwood was misled [by the signs].
Many of transport for London’s loading bays are incorrectly marked or signed.
Condition of signage and road markings
In a case in Oxford 828 the adjudicator found that although the lines were worn and no longer bright and fresh, they were none the less adequate to inform the motorist of the restriction and the motorists appeal was dismissed. However, in Plymouth 1477, also before NPAS, a motorist mistook a worn double yellow line for a single yellow line. The time plate was also obstructed by foliage. The adjudicator found that the road markings were not in adequate condition and allowed the appeal.
In Milton Keynes case number 329 before NPAS, a perfectly legible sign had been placed near a tree and became obscured by foliage during the summer months. Although the sign was in good condition and may well have been visible in the winter, it was found to be insufficiently visible during August when the trees were in full leaf and the appeal was allowed.

sections.heading: Yellow lines T-bar’s
sections.content:
In the case of Minier Vs London Borough of Camden, the motorist agued that the yellow line did not comply with the signage regulations because it did not have a T- bar at the point where it met parking bays. The adjudicator agreed that the traffic signs regulations and general directions 2002 (regulations) state that T-bars must be installed at the end of single and double yellow lines and that no permitted variants were allowed however, he also said that ‘the law does not concern itself with trifles and refused the motorists appeal. We would argue that if the markings are not in accordance with the regulations, they are not enforceable and as regards to ‘trifles’ we would equate this to enforcement of silly technical breaches of the parking regulations.
This case is also relevant to red routes as there may well be a T bar missing.

sections.heading: Disabled bays
sections.content:
CASE NO: 206 0450260 – Transport For London
North Kerb Bridge Place, SW1
“The Appellant parked his vehicle at the location in question and displayed his child’s disabled person’s badge.  Transport for London’s site photographs clearly established that parking for up to 3 hours is permitted at this location”.  Transport for London stated that waiting is only permitted between 10am and 4pm but they provided no evidence to support this and the Appeal was therefore upheld.
Quite unbelievable.

sections.heading: Waiting for a parking space or barrier.
sections.content:
Case 2070016726 – Contravention stopped where prohibited
This case concerned a vehicle that was stopped on a red route where prohibited. However the driver said that he was waiting for a pre-booked off street parking space to become vacant. His appeal was upheld because the adjudicator said “ this brings him within the exemption set out in article 9. (1) (e) of the traffic management order allowing waiting for the removal of a barrier at the entrance to the off street parking space”.
Always ask to see a copy of the traffic management order.
CASE NO 207 0010264 – Transport for London
Old Kent Road, London SE1
This case involved a disabled driver who had stopped behind a parking bay, got out of a vehicle asked the motorist sitting in the car in the disabled bay if he was about to move.  He said he was so she patiently waited with her hazard lights on although he did not move for a minute or two due to the fact that his car would not start.  The adjudicator stated that “It appears to me that [the Appellant] was causing her vehicle to be parked in the permitted box on the red route at the time alleged.  Causing a vehicle to be parked Paragraph 4 of the Traffic Management Order implies more than being parked and must include stopping and moving into a parking space.  That the parked cars should stall and delay the manoeuvre was not something that [the Appellant] could have predicted when she first arrived at the Old Kent Road.”  The Appeal was upheld.
It is interesting to note that the vehicle was illegally parked but was waiting for a parking space to be vacated by another car.

sections.heading: Mitigating circumstances/ compassionate grounds
sections.content:
CASE NO: 206 523772 – Transport for London
Tooley Street, London, SE1
The Appellant did not deny that the contravention had occurred but put forward mitigating factors to the local authority.  The adjudicator is not allowed to take into consideration mitigating factors but did refer the case back to the local authority to reconsider the “exercise of its discretion in light of the mitigation”.  The local authority was informed that if it did not respond the adjudicator would take the matter as being no longer contested.  Transport for London did not respond and the Appeal was therefore allowed.

sections.heading: Hired Vehicles
sections.content: In the case of ERAC Vs Ealing, a hired car received a penalty charge notice. The general rule is that a hire firm may transfer liability to the hirer provided certain conditions are satisfied as set out in the hiring agreement. The council tried to transfer liability for the payment of the PCN to the hiring firm because the address supplied for the hirer was outside the UK. The parking adjudicator found that it was irrelevant whether the address of the hirer was in this country or abroad, the legislation only provided that the permanent home address is given, unless the hirer was likely to stay in the country for more than 2 months from the date of the hiring. The appeal was allowed.
